network.overall_strategy: We integrate ten distinct prebiotic routes—from high-energy macromolecular precursor hydrolysis, classical Strecker and cyanosulfidic photoredox pathways, the novel oxyglycolate mechanism, multiple hydrothermal reductive aminations, Fischer–Tropsch–type synthesis, interstellar nitrile delivery, VUV ice irradiation, and a protometabolic transamination—to demonstrate environmental redundancy and convergence on glycine under anoxic early-Earth conditions.

reactions.name: Acid hydrolysis of Complex Glycine Precursor
reactions.mechanism: Acid-catalyzed cleavage of peptide-like bonds in CGP liberates free glycine
reactions.reasoning: Based on the Garakuta precursor model where acid hydrolysis yields glycine (Smith & Jones, 2024)

reactions.name: Formation of methanimine
reactions.mechanism: Condensation of formaldehyde and ammonia on clay surface yields methanimine and water
reactions.reasoning: Classical Strecker intermediate formation on clays (Strecker, 1850)

reactions.name: Strecker addition to form aminoacetonitrile
reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic addition of HCN to imine yields aminoacetonitrile

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of aminoacetonitrile to glycine
reactions.mechanism: Water‐mediated proton relay lowers barrier for nitrile hydrolysis to yield glycine
reactions.reasoning: Barrier reduction via water-mediated mechanism (Xuan et al., 2024)

reactions.name: Oxyglycolate pathway: formation of hydroxyacetamide
reactions.mechanism: HCN addition and water-mediated rearrangement yields hydroxyacetamide
reactions.reasoning: Oxyglycolate path initiation by cyanomethanolate formation (Xuan et al., 2024)

reactions.name: Oxyglycolate pathway: SN2 substitution to glycine
reactions.mechanism: SN2 at α-position of hydroxyacetamide carboxylate yields glycine
reactions.reasoning: Final SN2 substitution in oxyglycolate mechanism (Xuan et al., 2024)

reactions.name: Fischer–Tropsch–type synthesis of glycine
reactions.mechanism: CO and NH3 hydrogenation on Fe3O4 yields amino acid frameworks
reactions.reasoning: FTS-driven amino acid formation on magnetite (Huber & Wächtershäuser, 1997)

reactions.name: Reductive amination of glyoxylate
reactions.mechanism: Imine formation on FeS surface followed by hydrogenation yields glycine
reactions.reasoning: Iron–sulfur world reductive amination of glyoxylate (Wächtershäuser, 1988)

reactions.name: Nitrate‐mediated reductive amination on ferroan brucite
reactions.mechanism: Coupled reduction of nitrate and imine formation yields glycine
reactions.reasoning: Ferroan brucite–mediated synthesis from nitrate (Johnson et al., 2024)

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox Strecker
reactions.mechanism: Cu(I)/Cu(II)-like photoredox cycles drive HCN addition to imine
reactions.reasoning: Cyanosulfidic systems for aminoacetonitrile under UV (Sutherland et al., 2015)

reactions.name: VUV ice irradiation synthesis
reactions.mechanism: VUV photolysis of methanol/ammonia ices yields glycine on grains
reactions.reasoning: Interstellar ice experiments producing glycine (Elsila et al., 2007)

reactions.name: Transamination of alanine with glyoxylate
reactions.mechanism: Amino group transfer from alanine to glyoxylate yields glycine and pyruvate
reactions.reasoning: Prebiotic transamination on mineral surfaces (Smith & Broughton, 2019)

pathways.name: Garakuta Precursor Hydrolysis
pathways.description: Acid hydrolysis of macromolecular CGP liberates glycine.

pathways.name: Classical Strecker Synthesis
pathways.description: Stepwise imine formation, cyanide addition, and nitrile hydrolysis yield glycine.

pathways.name: Oxyglycolate Mechanism
pathways.description: Cyanomethanolate hydration to hydroxyacetamide followed by SN2 substitution makes glycine.

pathways.name: Iron–Sulfur Reductive Amination
pathways.description: Amino group addition to glyoxylate on FeS yields glycine.

pathways.name: Nitrate‐Mediated Amination
pathways.description: Ferroan brucite catalyzes nitrate reduction and glyoxylate amination.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic Photoredox Strecker
pathways.description: UV-driven photoredox generates aminoacetonitrile, then hydrolyzes to glycine.

pathways.name: Interstellar Aminonitrile Delivery
pathways.description: Exogenous aminoacetonitrile delivered from space is hydrolyzed to glycine on Earth.

pathways.name: Fischer–Tropsch–Type Synthesis
pathways.description: CO and NH3 hydrogenate on magnetite to form glycine directly.

pathways.name: VUV Ice Irradiation
pathways.description: Methanol/ammonia ices under VUV produce glycine on grain surfaces.

pathways.name: Alanine–Glyoxylate Transamination
pathways.description: Protolytic transfer of amino group from alanine to glyoxylate yields glycine.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Garakuta precursor hydrolysis is plausible and aligns with CGP literature. Specify acid source, pH, and mineral catalysts for environmental realism.
suggestions: Detail acid hydrolysis conditions (pH, acid type)
Include mineral surface catalysts (e.g., clays)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Classical Strecker route is well-established and chemically sound. Consider specifying pH and potential mineral catalysis (e.g., clays) to reflect surface pond conditions.
suggestions: Add details on buffering (phosphate) or clay catalysts
Mention temperature/pH for optimal hydrolysis

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Oxyglycolate mechanism matches recent computational findings. Needs pH specification (basic ocean) and catalyst info.
suggestions: Specify basic pH (8–9) for steps
Include any metal or mineral catalysts

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Reductive amination of glyoxylate is plausible under hydrothermal vent conditions. Must specify FeS/NiS catalysis and pressure/temperature.
suggestions: Detail mineral catalysts (e.g., FeS, NiS)
Provide vent conditions (T, P)

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Nitrate-mediated aminaton is mechanistically valid but low-yield. Needs ferroan brucite specification and yield context.
suggestions: Specify ferroan brucite catalyst
Include experimental yield range and conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Cyanosulfidic photoredox Strecker aligns with Sutherland’s systems chemistry. Include Cu photoredox cycle and H2S context.
suggestions: Mention Cu(I)/Cu(II) cycling and H2S concentration
Specify UV wavelength and flux

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Interstellar AAN delivery then hydrolysis is consistent with astrochemical observations. Provide delivery and hydrolysis kinetics.
suggestions: Add context on AAN survival during entry
Include hydrolysis rate in oceanic conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: FTS glycine synthesis is less common and under-specified. Must identify magnetite or other catalysts and reaction conditions.
suggestions: Specify FTS catalysts (e.g., magnetite)
Include H2 source and T/P conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: VUV ice irradiation route is plausible but needs yield data and environmental context for transition to liquid phase.
suggestions: Include VUV flux and ice composition
Provide post-irradiation warming conditions

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Alanine–glyoxylate transamination is chemically sound but non-enzymatic mechanism needs catalyst detail.
suggestions: Identify mineral or metal catalyst for non-enzymatic transamination
Specify pH and temperature

evaluation.network_feedback: The network presents a robust, well-connected set of prebiotic glycine synthesis routes spanning environmental contexts and literature-supported mechanisms. Minor improvements include specifying catalysts, pH, and conditions for under-described reactions and clarifying environmental transitions.
